runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for top U.S. earners, combining avoidance and real responses, with respect to the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Feldstein (1995) on taxable income responses to the 1986 Tax Reform Act
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) review on the elasticity of taxable income
Chetty (2009) on bounds/frictions in taxable income responses
runs.reasoningSummary: My central belief is that medium-run ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers is moderate, around 0.4, with substantial room for avoidance-driven responses and timing/income-shifting but not typically as high as the largest early estimates. The upper tail reflects periods or settings with especially strong avoidance opportunities; the lower tail reflects narrower real responses or stronger adjustment frictions.

The upper tail reflects periods or settings with especially strong avoidance opportunities; the lower tail reflects narrower real responses or stronger adjustment frictions."}

citations: Feldstein (1995) on taxable income responses to the 1986 Tax Reform Act
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) review of the elasticity of taxable income

runs.reasoningSummary: My central belief is that medium-run ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers is moderate rather than huge: large enough to reflect substantial avoidance and timing/margin responses, but usually below 1 once transitory effects and identification issues are accounted for. I place most mass around roughly 0.25 to 0.6, with a right tail for settings with especially strong avoidance opportunities.

runs.reasoningSummary: My central belief is that medium-run ETI for top U.S. earners is moderate: clearly above zero because avoidance, timing, income shifting, and some real labor/saving responses matter, but usually below very large early estimates once transitory responses and identification concerns are accounted for. citations: Feldstein (1995) on the Tax Reform Act and taxable income responsiveness

runs.reasoningSummary: My central belief is that medium-run ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers is moderate: clearly above zero, materially shaped by avoidance and timing as well as some real responses, and most plausibly around 0.4. runs.reasoningSummary: My memory is that medium-run ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers is commonly thought to be material but not huge, with central values around 0.3–0.5 once both avoidance and real responses are included.
